⌂ Lesson Library Italy: Constitutional Referendum
BBC News — 22 March 2026 — Sarah Rainsford
BELLA · Authentic Text

Italy is voting on whether to change its constitution

B2 — Upper Intermediate Grammar: Reporting Verbs Italian Politics · Current Affairs
The Italian Constitution, signed 27 December 1947 — the document at the heart of this debate
◆ ◆ ◆

Reading Passage

▶ Listen

In her push for a “yes” vote in this weekend’s constitutional referendum, Italy’s prime minister has been reaching for new audiences. On Thursday, Giorgia Meloni sat at the mic between a popular rapper and a personal trainer-turned-podcaster to urge Italians to vote for a judicial system she promised would be “more just” for all.

Opposition parties have been busy too, styling the “historic” reform proposed by the right-wing government as a threat to democracy, and turning it into a protest vote against Meloni’s rule. In power for three-and-a-half years, close to a record in a land of shaky coalitions, Meloni prides herself on bringing stability to Italy. But as this referendum has become increasingly personalised, she could be facing her first significant defeat.

“She wants to win. If she loses, there will be an impact and she understands that,” political scientist Roberto D’Alimonte of Luiss University explains. “She needs to mobilise her voters to have a better chance to win this race. But it’s going to be touch and go.”

So on air Meloni set out her case for “yes” with trademark gusto. She said changes to how Italy’s judges and prosecutors work would make the system “more meritocratic, more responsible, more efficient”. She also argued her opponents were only turning all this into a referendum on her rule because they couldn’t fault the reform itself. In any case, Meloni made clear, she wouldn’t be stepping down. “If you vote ‘no’ today just to send Meloni home, you’ll find yourself keeping both Meloni and a justice system that doesn’t work,” the prime minister said. “That doesn’t seem like a good deal to me.”

So is Italian justice really “broken”? The system is certainly chronically overloaded, with some of the longest-running trials in the EU. The referendum looks at separating the career paths of judges and prosecutors, as well as creating distinct bodies to govern them.

The proposal is a project of the late Silvio Berlusconi’s party, Forza Italia, part of the current coalition. Meloni herself has also clashed publicly with the courts, claiming that “powerful factions” routinely derail her signature policies, especially on migration. Such rhetoric is one reason why opposition parties are so wary of this reform. They fear Meloni’s real aim is to subjugate the judiciary to her political control. She and her team deny that.

But at a time of global uncertainty and rising prices, they also know voters may use the referendum to vent their frustration. “The geopolitical situation is very tough, of course. People are scared. I completely feel that. But we need to stay focused on the purpose of the referendum,” Andrea di Giuseppe, a member of Meloni’s Fratelli d’Italia party, argues.

There are some, even on the left wing of politics, who support this reform and see the close ties between judges and prosecutors as too cosy. On the other hand, this would be the first time a hard right government has changed Italy’s constitution, written in the 1940s after the defeat of fascism.

If Meloni wins this vote she has grand political ambitions, including increasing the powers of her own office in a future referendum. But the much-vaunted stability she has brought to Italy would be shaken at a particularly vulnerable time if she loses. “She will not resign,” the professor says. “But she will be politically weakened, there is no question about that.”

Language Notes

🇮🇹 Note per studenti italiani
Falsi amici chiave in questo testo:
• rhetoric / retorica — in inglese quasi sempre negativo (linguaggio vuoto o manipolativo); in italiano retorica può essere neutro o positivo.
• claims — in inglese implica scetticismo da parte dello scrittore. Non equivale a afferma / dice. Usarlo in modo improprio può far sembrare che il lettore dubiti della fonte.

Cognati sicuri: coalizione, meritocratico, cronicamente — stessa radice, stesso significato.

Collocazione da ricordare: in inglese shaky coalition, non weak coalition. In italiano coalizione fragile — l’aggettivo cambia.
🇫🇷 Notes pour les étudiants francophones
Faux amis clés dans ce texte:
• rhetoric / rhétorique — en anglais presque toujours négatif (langage creux ou manipulateur); en français rhétorique peut être neutre ou positif.
• claims — en anglais implique un scepticisme de la part de l’écrivain. Ne correspond pas à affirme / dit. L’utiliser à mauvais escient peut donner l’impression de douter de sa source.

Cognats sûrs: coalition, méritocratique, chroniquement — même racine, même sens.

Collocation à retenir: en anglais shaky coalition, pas weak coalition. En français coalition fragile — l’adjectif change.
🇪🇸 Notas para hispanohablantes
Falsos amigos clave en este texto:
• rhetoric / retórica — en inglés casi siempre negativo (lenguaje vacío o manipulador); en español retórica puede ser neutro o positivo.
• claims — en inglés implica escepticismo por parte del escritor. No equivale a afirma / dice. Usarlo mal puede dar la impresión de que se duda de la fuente.

Cognados seguros: coalición, meritocrático, crónicamente — misma raíz, mismo significado.

Colocación a recordar: en inglés shaky coalition, no weak coalition. En español coalición frágil — el adjetivo cambia.
◆ ◆ ◆

Grammar Spotlight

Words we use instead of ‘say’ — and why they matter

In journalism, writers use many different verbs to show what someone said. Each one sends a slightly different signal about how confident, aggressive, or neutral that person was being. At B2, it is worth building up a bank of these.

explains — the speaker is giving reasons or background calmly:

“D’Alimonte explains that she needs to mobilise her voters.”

argues — the speaker is making a case, pushing a point of view:

“Di Giuseppe argues that voters should focus on the referendum itself.”

claims — the speaker is asserting something, but the writer is slightly sceptical:

“Meloni claimed that ‘powerful factions’ routinely derail her policies.”

denies — the speaker is rejecting an accusation directly:

“She and her team deny that her aim is to control the judiciary.”

🇮🇹 Italiano: claims è utile da riconoscere, ma attenzione nell’usarlo. In inglese porta una sfumatura di dubbio — non è neutro come dice o afferma. Usare claims al posto di says nella propria scrittura può far sembrare di essere scettici sulla fonte.

🇫🇷 Français: claims est utile à reconnaître, mais prudence à l’utiliser. En anglais il porte une nuance de doute — ce n’est pas neutre comme dit ou affirme. L’utiliser à la place de says peut donner l’impression d’être sceptique envers sa source.

🇪🇸 Español: claims es útil para reconocer, pero cuidado al usarlo. En inglés conlleva una matiz de duda — no es neutro como dice o afirma. Usarlo en lugar de says puede dar la impresión de escepticismo hacia la fuente.

📚 Try this: find three more ‘says’ alternatives in the article
